Gladiator 
Description: The gladiator is 
a showman-warnor from a socl· 
ety where public combat competitions are a popular sport. 
The gladiatOr Is a professional 
warrior tn this high-profile 
arena: for the delight (and bloodlust) of the c.rowds. for his own 
personal wealth and aggran· 
dlzement (or, If he Is a slave. for 
the profits of his owner). he 
fights 
organized 
matches 
against human. demlhuman, 
and even monstrous opponents. 
There are no special ability' 
score requirements to be a Glad· 
lator. 
Role; For the Gladiator to ap· 
pear In a campaign. the OM 
must establish that at least one 
culture has gladiatorial com· 
bats, and the Gladiator character must come from such a 
culture. [He need not have been 
born there ... but he will either 
have been a slave there or. If he 
24 
was a freeman. wHl feel like a 
naturalized cItIzen there.} A 
Gladiator player·character can 
be a n acllve gladiator tn the 
arena. one who adventures In 
his free time (or within some 
other context of the current ad· 
venture), or can have formerly 
been a gladiator now living the 
life of the adventurer. 
In the campaign. the Gladiator Is going to be a showy, hJgh· 
profile warrior. He performs 
dangerous stunts in combat. He 
attracts the attention or crowds 
of admirers. He receives a lot of 
credit for brave deeds whether 
he deserves the credit or not. A 
Gladiator can be a callous brute, 
a dirty arena Oghter with no tn· 
terests other than kUling his enemy as quickly as possible and 
making off with his prize: or he 
can be a clean-Itmbed. heroic 
Ogure, a hero who always fights 
honorably in the arena and 
never kills when he does not 
have to. 
OMs take note: a Gladiator 
character Is not likely to be a 
Ranger. You can permit It If you 
wish, but Rangers are very 
wUderness-oriented characters. 
and Gladiators are very urban. 
A Ranger could have been cap· 
tured, enslaved, (rained as a 
Gladiator. and then escaped . .. 
but still. the Ranger and Gladiator personalities don't seem to 
work together very well. Allow 
this only if you really wish to. 
It's up to the DM to decide 
whether there are female gladia· 
tors on his world. Unless his 
campaign is already rigidly set 
up to pre\'ent It, he might as well 
allow It; a she-gladiator charac· 
ter could be a very Interesting 
one, 
Secondary Skills: The Gladiator character receives his sec· 
ondary skill through whatever 
means 
is 
usual 
for 
the 
campaign-by choice or ran· 
dam dle·roll. This skill probably 
represents the trade he learned 
before becoming a Gladiator.

Weapon ProCiciencies: Required: short sword {glad Ius). 
trident. net. Gladiators should 
learn an even mix of nonnal and 
unusual weapons; the OM Is 
within his rights to Insisl thaI 
the Gladiator learn one strange 
weapon proficiency (such as 
whip) for every "normal'- proficiency {Uke sword, spear. etc.}. 
(Also. see Lhe Equipment chapl er, u nder "New Arms" and 
"New Annar:' for weapons and 
armor especially appropriate to 
Gladiator characters.) 
Nonweapon Proficiencies: 
Bonus Proficlencles: (Warrior) 
Cha rloteerlng. (Rogue) Thmhllng (for the combat showmanship mat characterizes arena 
fighting). Recommended: (General) Animal Handling. Animal 
Training. 
Eliq uette. 
Riding 
(Land-Based), 
(Warrior) 
Armorer. Bllnd-Flghllng. Endurance. 
Gaming, 
Weaponsmithing. (Priest) Healing (double slots unless Paladin). 
Equipment: The Gladiator 
may buy any son of non· 
magical weapon or combination 
of weapons before beginning 
play. However. he must choose 
his armor from lhe listing of 
Gladiator Armor in lhe Equip· 
ment chapter. under "New Armors:' 
Special Benefits: Gladiators. 
because of meir intensive trainIng, get a free Weapon Specialization (sec under " Weapon 
SpecializatiOn" In the 2nd Edition Player's Handbook) , This 
doesn't cost any of their beginning 
weapon 
proflciencles: 
They still get all four of those, 
and gellhls Specialization free. 
It must be chosen from one of 
the following weapons: bow 
(chOice). cestus·, dagger. drusus", lasso·. nel- , scimitar. 
short sword. spear, trident. and 
whip. (The" - " indicates a new 
weapon found in the Equipmenl 
chapler.) 
Special Hindrances: Gladiators tend to be recognized-as 
Gladiators. at least. if not by 
their own names-where\rer 
they go. This makes It more difficult for them to do lhlngs in secrct; some troublesome NPC is 
always remembering "'the tall. 
fair-haired gladiator" who was at 
the scene of the action. which 
makes iL very easy for the au· 
thorlties to follow the heroes' 
trail. (This Is something the OM 
will have to enforce scrupulously 
if the Gladiator is to have hin· 
drances offscttlng his benents.l 
Also. and this Is strictly a roleplaying consideration, promoters and managers are 
aJways interfering In the Gladiator's life: Trying to hire him to 
participate In certain-death 
events, to fighl people the Gladiator doesn't wam to fight. to 
force him to participate In 
events taking place at the exact 
time the Gladiator needs to be 
some ..... here else, etc. These promoters will go to any length to 
get their way; they may blackmail the characler. kidnap his 
followers. use the time-honored 
baH of a gorgeous romantic Interest (whom the Gladiator 
doesn't Immedlalely realize Is 
an employee of the promoter), 
and so forth. 
lb make sure this is regarded 
as a hindrance. the OM should 
make It clear that these promoters arc mostly of the sleazy 
variety who will cheat. rob and 
betray him at the drop of a hal. 
Wealth Options: The Gladiator gelS the standard 5d4x 10 gp 
to spend. and may spend It any 
way he chooses (subject to the 
restrictions listed In "Equipment." Immediately above) or 
have It all unspent at the beginning of play. 
Races: Any demihuman war· 
rior can be a Gladiator. Opera· 
tors of the arenas lry to acquire 
as many dlffc rent, unusual 
flghters as Ihey can. by hiring or 
enslaving them, and dcmlhumans (when they can be ac· 
qulred) are major attractions.

MyrmldQR. 
Description: The Mynnldon 
Is the ultimate soldier. SoldierIng 15 his life. He may be a highranking officer or a career 
sergeant; he may belong to one 
nation's armed forces or may be 
a mercenary. Th the campaign 
and the adventuring party, he 
brings diSCipline and a useful 
understanding of military tactics: he's often rigid and contemptuous 
of 
rugged 
individualists or characters who 
don't like to take orders. so he 
can cause a lot of frieLion in an 
adventuring party. too. 
When first created, the Myrmldon's player must decide 
whether his character Is part of 
a standing army or a mercenary 
unit. Ir he's part of a standing 
anny. he's employed as a soldier 
or omcer In the army of a nation, 
large region. city guard. or even 
palace/castle guard. If he's pan 
of a mercenary unit, he belongs 
to a group of freelance soldiers 
who hire themselves to just 
about anyone who can pay: or 
may be a personal bodyguard. 
The OM will have the deciding 
VOLe In what sort of force the 
Myrmidon belongs to: If. for in· 
stance. the OM doesn't want to 
have an ail-military campaign, 
he' ll probably Insis t that the 
Myrmidon be a mercenary. cur· 
rently employed by a playercharacter or NPC Important to 
the current story. 
However. In the course of the 
campaign. the Myrmidon's employment can change. once or 
several times. He may start out 
as a mercenary bodyguard: 
later In the campaign. he may 
find himself commanding a 
small mercenary force In a border war: later still. he may accept a commission In the klng's 
army and find himself a regular 
officer. 
The choice of whether the 
character Is of a non-commlssloned rank (such as recruit. prl-

o 
vale. or sergeantl or an officer's 
rank (such as Caplaln) Is entirely 
up to the OM, who'll make his 
c hoice based on what works 
best In his campaign's current 
storyline.
